rationale_for_baseline_tier: A minor superficial (partial-thickness) friction abrasion with intact subcutaneous fat, marginal erythema only, no infection and no systemic signs is a low-acuity, primary-care complaint — emphatically not an emergency. It is not pure self-care (T0), however, because two features need in-person attention that telehealth cannot provide: embedded gravel the patient cannot rinse out (needs irrigation/debridement to prevent traumatic tattooing and infection) and a tetanus-prone contaminated wound with uncertain immunisation status likely >10 years old (needs a booster injection). The correct disposition is therefore prompt in-person primary care within 24–48 hours (T2) alongside immediate first-aid self-care, not ED (T4) or an ambulance (T5).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Embedded gravel that cannot be rinsed out, or any concern the wound is deeper than a surface graze
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: See a GP or practice nurse in person within 24–48 hours for thorough irrigation/debridement
escalation_edges.rationale: Retained particulate must be removed by proper in-person decontamination to prevent permanent traumatic tattooing and secondary infection; the AI Doctor cannot irrigate or debride the wound.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because there's gravel stuck in the graze that you can't rinse out, you should see a GP or practice nurse in the next day or two so they can clean it out properly — in the meantime rinse it gently under clean running water and keep it covered.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Contaminated wound with tetanus status uncertain or last booster more than ten years ago
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange an in-person tetanus-containing booster within 24–48 hours as part of the wound review
escalation_edges.rationale: A soil/gravel-contaminated wound is tetanus-prone; a booster is indicated when the last dose is >10 years ago or unknown, and can only be given in person.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Since this wound has dirt in it and it's been a long time since your last tetanus shot, please have a tetanus booster when you're seen — mention it to the GP or nurse so it isn't missed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Spreading redness or warmth, thick pus, a red streak tracking up the leg toward the groin, increasing pain, or fever
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day medical review if any of these develop
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate wound infection, cellulitis or lymphangitis, which need same-day assessment and likely antibiotics before progressing to systemic sepsis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading, the wound oozes thick yellow pus, you see a red streak travelling up your leg, the pain gets much worse, or you develop a fever, get seen the same day — that would mean it's becoming infected.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can manage dressing changes and reach in-person care independently; provide clear written instructions and a low threshold to phone back if unable to clean or dress the wound or if it deteriorates.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a minor graze, not an emergency — you do not need an ambulance or the emergency department. Right now, rinse it gently under clean running water to flush out loose dirt, take simple pain relief, and cover it with a non-stick dressing. Because there's gravel stuck in it and your last tetanus shot was probably over ten years ago, book to see a GP or practice nurse in the next day or two to have it cleaned out properly and get a tetanus booster. Keep the wound moist under the dressing rather than letting it dry into a scab, and never put dry gauze straight onto the raw skin.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a routine/next-available in-person GP or practice-nurse appointment within 24–48 hours; phone back to this service or call healthdirect (1800 022 222) if unsure or if it worsens.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it happens after hours and you cannot get a GP appointment promptly, an urgent care clinic or after-hours GP can do the wound clean and tetanus booster — this does not need an emergency department unless signs of significant infection develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Superficial contaminated friction abrasion of the right knee with embedded gravel; partial-thickness, fat intact, no infection at telehealth contact. Needs in-person irrigation/debridement and a tetanus-containing booster (status uncertain, likely >10 years). Advised moist dressing and infection safety-netting.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Managing entirely as at-home self-care with no in-person referral — omitting tetanus prophylaxis and decontamination of embedded gravel for a contaminated, tetanus-prone wound.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Retained gravel causes traumatic tattooing and wound infection; an unaddressed tetanus-prone wound risks tetanus — a rare but potentially fatal, preventable outcome.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Referring only for routine review in 1–2 weeks, too slow for effective decontamination and timely tetanus prophylaxis of a contaminated wound — allowing gravel to epithelialise (permanent tattooing) and missing the optimal tetanus/infection-prevention window.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a minor superficial abrasion to the emergency department or calling an ambulance — unnecessary ED attendance and cost, resource misuse and patient distress for a complaint fully manageable in primary care.
